standard.notes: 'En ville' = in town / in the city (no article needed). 'Aller en ville' = to go into town. 'Le centre-ville' = the city centre. 'Une grande ville' = a large city. 'Une petite ville' = a small town. France has over 35,000 communes — 'la ville' typically implies a more urban settlement.
examples: J'habite dans une grande ville.
I live in a large city.
Nous allons en ville ce week-end.
We are going into town this weekend.

standard.notes: 'Faire les magasins' = to go shopping (visiting several shops). 'Un grand magasin' = a department store (e.g. Galeries Lafayette). 'Ouvrir / fermer le magasin' = to open / close the shop. 'En magasin' = in store (as opposed to online).
examples: Il y a beaucoup de magasins dans le centre.
There are many shops in the centre.
Le magasin est fermé le dimanche.
The shop is closed on Sunday.

standard.notes: 'Aller à l'église' = to go to church. 'La messe' = the mass. Churches are major landmarks in French cities and villages. Notre-Dame de Paris and Sacré-Cœur are famous examples. 'Devant l'église' = in front of the church (common meeting point).
examples: L'église est au centre du village.
The church is in the centre of the village.
On se retrouve devant l'église à dix heures.
We'll meet in front of the church at 10.

standard.notes: 'La fontaine' is a common landmark in French town squares and parks. 'La Fontaine de Trevi' = the Trevi Fountain (Rome). French towns often have historic ornamental fountains. 'Une fontaine d'eau potable' = a drinking fountain. Used as landmarks: 'C'est près de la fontaine' = It's near the fountain.
examples: Il y a une belle fontaine sur la place principale.
There is a beautiful fountain on the main square.
On se retrouve à la fontaine.
We'll meet at the fountain.

standard.notes: 'Le jardin public' = the public garden / park. 'Le Jardin du Luxembourg' is one of Paris's most famous parks. 'Faire du jardinage' = to do gardening. 'Le jardin zoologique' = the zoo. 'Un jardin à la française' = a formal French garden (geometrical style).
examples: Le jardin du palais est magnifique.
The palace garden is magnificent.
On peut pique-niquer dans le jardin public.
We can have a picnic in the public garden.

advanced.notes: 'En ville' = in town / in the city (no article needed). 'Aller en ville' = to go into town. 'Le centre-ville' = the city centre. 'Une grande ville' = a large city. 'Une petite ville' = a small town. France has over 35,000 communes — 'la ville' typically implies a more urban settlement.
examples: J'habite dans une grande ville.
I live in a large city.
Nous allons en ville ce week-end.
We are going into town this weekend.

examples: Il y a beaucoup de magasins dans le centre.
There are many shops in the centre.
Le magasin est fermé le dimanche.
The shop is closed on Sunday.

examples: L'église est au centre du village.
The church is in the centre of the village.
On se retrouve devant l'église à dix heures.
We'll meet in front of the church at 10.

advanced.notes: 'La fontaine' is a common landmark in French town squares and parks. 'La Fontaine de Trevi' = the Trevi Fountain (Rome). French towns often have historic ornamental fountains. 'Une fontaine d'eau potable' = a drinking fountain. Used as landmarks: 'C'est près de la fontaine' = It's near the fountain.
examples: Il y a une belle fontaine sur la place principale.
There is a beautiful fountain on the main square.
On se retrouve à la fontaine.
We'll meet at the fountain.

advanced.notes: 'Le jardin public' = the public garden / park. 'Le Jardin du Luxembourg' is one of Paris's most famous parks. 'Faire du jardinage' = to do gardening. 'Le jardin zoologique' = the zoo. 'Un jardin à la française' = a formal French garden (geometrical style).
examples: Le jardin du palais est magnifique.
The palace garden is magnificent.
On peut pique-niquer dans le jardin public.
We can have a picnic in the public garden.

advanced.notes: 'Visiter un musée' = to visit a museum. France is home to some of the world's most visited museums — the Louvre is the most visited museum globally. 'Le musée est gratuit le premier dimanche' = The museum is free on the first Sunday (many French national museums offer free entry on the first Sunday of the month).
examples: Le musée est ouvert du mardi au dimanche.
The museum is open from Tuesday to Sunday.
En France, on peut visiter beaucoup de musées gratuits.
In France, you can visit many free museums.

advanced.meaning: the city centre / the town centre
advanced.notes: 'Habiter en centre-ville' = to live in the city centre. 'Le commerce en centre-ville' = city-centre retail. In France, town centres typically have covered markets, pedestrian zones ('les zones piétonnes'), and historic buildings. 'La revitalisation du centre-ville' is a frequent topic in French urban planning.
examples: Le marché est au centre-ville.
The market is in the town centre.
J'habite en centre-ville, près de la fontaine.
I live in the city centre, near the fountain.

dialogue: Nous venons d'Australie — c'est notre première visite en France !
Bienvenue ! Qu'est-ce que vous voulez voir à Paris ?
En France, on peut visiter beaucoup de musées, non ?
Oui ! À Paris, il y a le Louvre et beaucoup de jardins magnifiques.
Et des fontaines ? En France, les fontaines sont célèbres !
Oui, il y en a partout ! Et il y a aussi de belles églises.
Super — nous voulons tout voir. Par où commencer ?

questions.question: What famous museum does speaker A mention?
What does speaker B say about fountains?
options: They say French fountains are famous
questions.correctAnswer: They say French fountains are famous

standard.summary: Friends visiting from Australia are on their first trip to France. They ask about what to see in Paris. Speaker A recommends the Louvre, the gardens, and the churches, and notes there are fountains everywhere. The visitors are enthusiastic about seeing everything and wonder where to start.

dialogue: Oui, j'habite à Bordeaux. Et vous, vous venez d'où ?
Nous venons du Japon — c'est notre troisième visite en France.
Ah, vous connaissez bien ! Vous avez déjà visité beaucoup de villes ?
Oui — nous sommes allés à Paris, à Lyon, et maintenant à Bordeaux. En France, chaque ville a son caractère.
C'est vrai ! À Bordeaux, il y a de magnifiques jardins, des musées excellents, et une belle fontaine au centre-ville.
Oui, nous avons vu la fontaine hier. Et les magasins en centre-ville sont très sympas.
Exactement. Au Japon, dans quelle ville habitez-vous ?

questions.question: Which cities have the visitors already seen?
What did the visitors see yesterday?
What does speaker B say about Bordeaux?
options: It has gardens, museums and a fountain in the centre
It has the best shops in France
questions.correctAnswer: It has gardens, museums and a fountain in the centre

advanced.summary: Speaker A and their companion are visiting from Japan — it is their third trip to France. They have already visited Paris and Lyon, and are now in Bordeaux. Speaker B (a Bordeaux resident) recommends the gardens, museums, and the central fountain. The visitors already saw the fountain yesterday and enjoyed the city-centre shops. Speaker B asks which city the visitors are from in Japan.

standard.title: Prepositions with Cities & Countries (à, en, au, aux)
standard.explanation:
In French, the preposition you use before a city or country name depends on the gender and number of the place.

**With cities:** always use **à**
- J'habite **à** Paris. (I live in Paris.)
- Je vais **à** Lyon. (I'm going to Lyon.)

**With countries:**
| Country type | Preposition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Feminine country | **en** | en France, en Espagne, en Italie |
| Masculine country (starts with consonant) | **au** | au Japon, au Portugal, au Canada |
| Masculine country (starts with vowel) | **en** | en Iran, en Irak |
| Plural country | **aux** | aux États-Unis, aux Pays-Bas |

**How to know if a country is feminine:** Most countries ending in **-e** are feminine (la France, l'Italie, l'Espagne). Exceptions: le Mexique, le Cambodge, le Zimbabwe.

**Common examples:**
- Je viens **de** France. (I come from France.)
- Elle habite **au** Japon. (She lives in Japan.)
- Ils sont **aux** États-Unis. (They are in the US.)

examples.english: I live in Paris, in France.

examples.french: Il vient au Japon — il habite à Tokyo.
examples.english: He is going to Japan — he lives in Tokyo.

examples.french: Nous allons aux États-Unis cet été.
examples.english: We are going to the United States this summer.

examples.french: En Italie, il y a de belles fontaines.
examples.english: In Italy, there are beautiful fountains.

drills.question: How do you say 'in France'?
drills.explanation: France is feminine (la France). Feminine countries use 'en': en France.

drills.question: Complete: J'habite ___ Paris. (I live in Paris.)
drills.explanation: Cities always use 'à'. J'habite à Paris.

drills.question: How do you say 'in Japan'?
drills.explanation: Japan is masculine (le Japon). Masculine countries starting with consonant: au Japon.

drills.question: Complete: Ils habitent ___ États-Unis. (They live in the United States.)
drills.explanation: Plural country: use 'aux'. Les États-Unis → aux États-Unis.

drills.question: Which preposition do you use with feminine countries?
drills.explanation: Feminine countries (most ending in -e) use 'en': en France, en Italie, en Espagne.

advanced.title: Prepositions with Cities & Countries — à, en, au, aux + Exceptions
advanced.explanation:
**Cities: always à**
- *à Paris, à Lyon, à Tokyo, à New York*
- *Je vais **à** Madrid.* (I'm going to Madrid.)

**Countries — preposition depends on gender and number:**
| Type | Prep (in/to) | Prep (from) | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feminine (ending -e) | **en** | **de** | en France, de France |
| Masc. (consonant) | **au** | **du** | au Japon, du Japon |
| Masc. (vowel) | **en** | **d'** | en Iran, d'Iran |
| Plural | **aux** | **des** | aux États-Unis, des États-Unis |

**Feminine country exceptions (masculine despite ending in -e):**
- *le Mexique* → **au** Mexique
- *le Cambodge* → **au** Cambodge
- *le Zimbabwe* → **au** Zimbabwe

**Islands and regions vary — learn as fixed phrases:**
- *à Cuba, à Haïti, à Malte* (small islands: use à)
- *en Corse* (larger island, treated as region: en)
- *en Provence, en Normandie* (French regions: en)
- *dans le Midi* (some regions with 'le': dans le)

**With 'from' (venir de):**
- *Je viens **de** France.* (de = from feminine country)
- *Je viens **du** Japon.* (du = de + le)
- *Je viens **des** États-Unis.* (des = de + les)
- *Je viens **d'**Australie.* (d' = de + vowel)

**Key contrast — city vs country:**
- *J'habite **à** Paris **en** France.*
- *Il est **au** Japon — **à** Tokyo.*

examples.french: Elle habite à Bordeaux, en France — elle vient du Japon.
examples.english: She lives in Bordeaux, France — she comes from Japan.

examples.french: En Provence, il y a de beaux jardins et des fontaines magnifiques.
examples.english: In Provence, there are beautiful gardens and magnificent fountains.

examples.french: Ils viennent des États-Unis mais ils habitent à Paris.
examples.english: They come from the United States but they live in Paris.

drills.question: How do you say 'in Australia'?
drills.explanation: Australia is feminine (l'Australie, ends in -e). Feminine countries: en. Also starts with vowel so 'en' is confirmed.

drills.question: Complete: Je viens ___ Japon. (I come from Japan.)
drills.explanation: 'De + le Japon = du Japon'. Venir du Japon = to come from Japan.

drills.question: How do you say 'in Mexico'?
drills.explanation: Le Mexique is a masculine exception (ends in -e but is masculine). Use 'au': au Mexique.

drills.question: Complete: Elle vient ___ États-Unis. (She comes from the United States.)
drills.explanation: 'De + les États-Unis = des États-Unis'. Plural countries use 'des' for 'from'.

drills.question: How do you say 'in Corsica'?
drills.explanation: Corse is treated as a region in French. Regions use 'en': en Corse, en Provence.

drills.question: Complete: J'habite ___ Tokyo, ___ Japon. (I live in Tokyo, Japan.)
drills.explanation: Cities use 'à' (à Tokyo); masculine countries use 'au' (au Japon).

drills.question: Which is correct: 'I come from France'?
drills.explanation: 'De' + feminine country (no article needed): je viens de France. NOT 'du' (which is de + le, for masculine).

drills.question: Complete: En Provence, ___ y a de beaux jardins. (In Provence, there are beautiful gardens.)
drills.explanation: 'Il y a' = there is/are. En Provence, il y a de beaux jardins.

standard.question: How do you say 'in France'?
standard.explanation: Feminine countries use 'en'. France is feminine (la France) → en France.

standard.question: What does 'le jardin' mean?
standard.explanation: 'Le jardin' = the garden. 'Le jardin public' = the public garden / park.

standard.question: Complete: J'habite ___ Paris. (I live in Paris.)
standard.explanation: Cities always use 'à': j'habite à Paris.

standard.question: What does 'l'église' mean?
standard.explanation: 'L'église' = the church. A common landmark used for meetings: 'devant l'église'.

standard.question: How do you say 'in Japan'?
standard.explanation: Japan is masculine (le Japon), starting with a consonant → au Japon.

advanced.question: What does 'le centre-ville' mean?
advanced.explanation: 'Le centre-ville' = the city/town centre. 'Habiter en centre-ville' = to live in the city centre.

advanced.question: Complete: Je viens ___ États-Unis. (I come from the United States.)
advanced.explanation: 'De + les États-Unis = des États-Unis'. Plural countries use 'des' for 'from'.

advanced.question: How do you say 'in Mexico'?
advanced.explanation: Le Mexique is masculine (exception — ends in -e but masculine). Use 'au': au Mexique.

advanced.question: What does 'le musée' mean?
advanced.explanation: 'Le musée' = the museum. France has many world-famous museums, including the Louvre.

advanced.question: Complete: J'habite ___ Tokyo, ___ Japon. (I live in Tokyo, Japan.)
advanced.explanation: Cities use 'à' (à Tokyo); masculine countries use 'au' (au Japon).

advanced.question: How do you say 'in Corsica'?
advanced.explanation: Corsica is treated as a French region → en Corse. Same for en Provence, en Normandie.

standard.scenario: Describe your city or neighbourhood to a French pen pal. Mention where things are located (churches, gardens, fountains, shops) and say which country and city you live in. Use the correct prepositions with your city and country.
keyPhrases: J'habite à… en… / au… (I live in [city], in [country])
Dans ma ville, il y a… (In my town, there is…)
L'église est près de la fontaine. (The church is near the fountain.)
Il y a beaucoup de magasins en centre-ville. (There are many shops in the city centre.)
standard.modelAnswer: Bonjour ! J'habite à Sydney, en Australie. C'est une grande ville au bord de la mer. Dans ma ville, il y a de beaux jardins et beaucoup de magasins en centre-ville. Il y a aussi une grande église près de la fontaine principale. En Australie, il fait souvent chaud et il y a beaucoup à visiter. Tu peux venir un jour !
standard.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer uses 'à' + city (à Sydney), 'en' + feminine country (en Australie), describes the city with 'il y a', references key vocabulary (jardins, magasins, église, fontaine), and invites the pen pal to visit.
standard.tip: Remember: cities = à; feminine countries (usually ending in -e) = en; masculine countries = au; plural countries = aux.

advanced.scenario: You are chatting with a French friend about cities you have visited and where you live now. Use 'à', 'en', 'au', 'aux' correctly with multiple cities and countries. Compare your city to a French city, and mention landmarks like museums, gardens, and fountains.
keyPhrases: J'habite à… en / au / aux… (I live in [city], in [country])
Je viens de / du / des… (I come from…)
En France / Au Japon / Aux États-Unis, il y a… (In France / Japan / the US, there is…)
Comme à Paris, il y a… (Like in Paris, there is…)
Le centre-ville a… / Le musée est… (The city centre has… / The museum is…)
advanced.modelAnswer: J'habite à Melbourne, en Australie — mais je viens du Japon, de Tokyo. C'est intéressant : à Melbourne, il y a de très beaux jardins, comme le Jardin du Luxembourg à Paris. Il y a aussi des musées excellents et une grande fontaine dans le centre-ville. En Australie, les magasins ferment plus tôt qu'en France. Toi, tu habites à Bordeaux ? En France, chaque ville a son caractère — comme au Japon !
advanced.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer uses 'à' + city (three cities), 'en' + feminine country (en Australie, en France), 'du' + masculine country (du Japon), makes a cross-cultural comparison, and uses key vocabulary (jardins, musées, fontaine, centre-ville, magasins).
